reasoning: The 90-day moving average of SOFR is steadily decreasing because daily SOFR rates currently entering the 90-day window (~3.63%) are lower than those falling out from late December (~3.65-3.68%). More importantly, shifting the 90-day window from March 29 to April 5 drops the final days of December, including the End-of-Year (EOY) spike on December 31. EOY spikes are historically the largest spikes of the year, and in 2025 it carried a 2-day weight because January 1 was a holiday. The days being added include March 31 (End-of-Quarter), which will have a much smaller spike and only single-day weighting. Because the baseline trend is downward and we are replacing a massive dual-day EOY spike with a small single-day EOQ spike, the 90-day average is mathematically bounded to decrease barring a catastrophic and unprecedented multi-day repo market breakdown in late March. Thus, the probability of an increase is functionally minimal.
